title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses a smuggling operation with Inspector Waddington and an inspector from Bell. Poirot categorizes the smuggling problem into three areas: the distribution of drugs, the methods used to enter the country, and the identity of the mastermind. The Bell inspector notes that while distribution channels include pharmacies, nightclubs, and various service providers, authorities are struggling to track the increasing rate of drug entry. He also mentions concerns regarding the smuggling of precious stones from the Far East, Australia, and South Africa.

Poirot theorizes that smugglers exploit a flaw in the shipping process to transport small, high-value items like cocaine, heroin, or gems. He suggests they use unwitting carriers, specifically students, to avoid suspicion. Poirot describes a specific scheme involving high-quality, durable backpacks sold on the market. These bags feature a thick, removable lining and a slight difference in stitching at the bottom, which is used to hide contraband. A group, potentially led by a student or someone posing as one, uses these bags to smuggle goods across the English Channel, replacing the tampered bag or lining upon their return to Britain to evade customs.

Poirot links this scheme to Walnut Street, noting that the backpacks sold there were suspiciously cheap. He deduces that a backpack was cut into pieces and discarded in a boiler room garbage bin because a smuggler, fearing police surveillance following an unrelated visit to a dormitory supervisor, felt compelled to destroy the evidence. Poirot believes the act of cutting the bag was a desperate attempt to prevent the item from being traced back to illegal substances.
